extracted_rules.rule_name: Concurrent HIIT + resistance — preserve musculoskeletal function
extracted_rules.condition: If program combines HIIT and resistance training over 8-12 weeks
extracted_rules.action: Design the program to improve or maintain maximal strength, explosive performance, neuromuscular activation, and muscle size simultaneously
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Program duration: 8-12 weeks; resistance sessions: 2-3/week; HIIT sessions: 2-3/week; interference minimal when structured correctly
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: concurrent HIIT and resistance training for musculoskeletal function

extracted_rules.rule_name: Sprint interval training (SIT) — no interference on strength or power
extracted_rules.condition: If SIT (sprint intervals) is integrated into a concurrent training program with resistance training
extracted_rules.action: Do not expect reduction in strength or power gains; SIT improves cardiorespiratory fitness without compromising resistance training adaptations
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: SIT + RT: no significant difference in strength/power vs RT alone; cardiorespiratory fitness improves with SIT addition
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: does SIT cause interference in concurrent training

extracted_rules.rule_name: Short sprint protocols preserve jump performance
extracted_rules.condition: If goal is to maintain or improve jump performance (vertical/horizontal power) in a concurrent training program
extracted_rules.action: Include sprint protocols of ≤10 seconds duration; longer sprint protocols may impair jump performance adaptations
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Sprint duration ≤10 seconds: jump performance maintained or improved; >10s sprints: potential interference with power adaptations
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: SIT and interference in concurrent training

extracted_rules.rule_name: Heavy strength training improves endurance cycling economy
extracted_rules.condition: If an endurance cyclist (or triathlete) adds heavy resistance training to their program
extracted_rules.action: Expect improvement in cycling economy and endurance performance; heavy strength training does not cause interference in trained cyclists
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Heavy RT (≥85% 1RM, 3-5 sets, 2-4 reps) 2-3×/week: improves economy; no significant VO2max or LT interference in trained cyclists
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review with meta-analysis: heavy strength training effects on endurance cyclist performance

extracted_rules.rule_name: Session order — strength before endurance (same day)
extracted_rules.condition: If strength and endurance sessions must be performed on the same day
extracted_rules.action: Schedule strength session first, followed by the endurance session; this order minimizes interference with strength adaptations
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Same-day ordering: Strength → Endurance; reverse order (Endurance → Strength) reduces maximal strength adaptation by ~10-15%
extracted_rules.source: Coffey & Hawley 2017; concurrent training interference research consensus

extracted_rules.condition: If strength and endurance sessions are on the same day and session order matters
extracted_rules.action: Separate strength and endurance sessions by at least 6 hours to allow partial recovery and minimize interference
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Minimum gap: 6 hours between strength and endurance sessions; 8 hours preferred for high-intensity sessions
extracted_rules.source: Coffey & Hawley 2017; Baar 2014 molecular interference review

extracted_rules.condition: When selecting the endurance modality for a concurrent training day
extracted_rules.action: Prefer cycling over running for same-day combination with heavy lifting; running causes greater leg-muscle interference than cycling
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Interference severity (on hypertrophy/strength): running > cycling > swimming; cycling preferred when combining with lower-body lifting
extracted_rules.source: Wilson et al. 2012 meta-analysis on concurrent training interference

extracted_rules.rule_name: Endurance-first ordering when gap is <6 hours
extracted_rules.condition: If strength and endurance sessions must be separated by less than 6 hours AND the priority goal is hypertrophy
extracted_rules.action: When gap is unavoidably <6h and hypertrophy is priority, consider endurance first to have fresher muscles for the strength session
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Gap <6h + hypertrophy priority: Endurance → Strength preferred; otherwise default to Strength → Endurance
extracted_rules.source: Baar 2014 molecular signaling review; emerging concurrent training literature

extracted_rules.condition: If designing a concurrent training program for an endurance athlete
extracted_rules.action: Prescribe 2-3 resistance training sessions per week; more than 3 increases interference risk without additional adaptation benefit
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Resistance sessions: 2-3/week; endurance sessions: 3-6/week depending on sport; total sessions: ≤9-10/week to manage fatigue
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: concurrent HIIT and resistance for musculoskeletal function

extracted_rules.condition: If explaining why same-day session ordering and gaps matter
extracted_rules.action: Note that endurance exercise activates AMPK (catabolic signaling) which suppresses mTOR (anabolic signaling) for 3-6 hours; this is the molecular basis for the 6h gap rule
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: AMPK activation post-endurance: peak 0-2h, resolves 3-6h; mTOR suppression window: 0-6h post-endurance; strength first avoids this suppression during the critical 2-3h post-strength window
extracted_rules.source: Baar 2014 molecular review; Hawley et al. 2016 concurrent training signaling

extracted_rules.condition: If a hybrid athlete needs active recovery on a high-stress week without adding CNS or mechanical load
extracted_rules.action: Prescribe FIRST-style non-weight-bearing cross-training (cycling, swimming) as interference-free active recovery; preserves aerobic load without adding musculoskeletal stress
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Non-weight-bearing XT: cycling or pool running; CNS load ≈ 0; mechanical stress ≈ 0; aerobic load maintained; use on days where running interference with strength is a concern
extracted_rules.source: Pierce, Murr & Moss, Run Less Run Faster (FIRST); cross-training interference research

extracted_rules.rule_name: Strength before long run — Pfitz sequencing
extracted_rules.condition: If a heavy lower-body strength session and a long run are scheduled within 24 hours of each other
extracted_rules.action: Always perform the strength session before the long run, not after; residual fatigue from a long run degrades strength quality more than the reverse
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Order: Strength -> (rest ≥6h or next morning) -> Long Run; reverse order (Long Run -> Strength) reduces peak force output by 15-25% in the strength session
extracted_rules.source: Pfitzinger & Douglas, Advanced Marathoning; concurrent training sequencing meta-analysis

extracted_rules.condition: When designing an annual training plan for a hybrid endurance-strength athlete
extracted_rules.action: Consider alternating 4-week strength-emphasis blocks and 4-week endurance-emphasis blocks rather than concurrent year-round programming; reduces chronic interference vs simultaneous high loads
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Strength block: 3-4 sessions/week at MAV-MRV; endurance at MEV; Endurance block: 4-6 sessions/week at peak volume; strength at MEV (maintenance); transition week: 1 deload between blocks
extracted_rules.source: Issurin 2010 block periodization theory; concurrent training interference meta-analysis

extracted_rules.condition: During the taper phase (final 2-3 weeks before A-race)
extracted_rules.action: Reduce strength training to MEV (maintenance); maintain intensity, reduce volume by 40-60%; avoid DOMS-inducing sessions within 7 days of A-race
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Taper strength: MEV sets/week (8-10 sets/muscle group); load unchanged; volume cut 40-60%; no novel exercises; no eccentric-heavy work (Nordic curls, tempo squats) within 7 days of race
extracted_rules.source: Concurrent training taper consensus; Pfitzinger & Douglas, Advanced Marathoning (taper principles applied to strength)

extracted_rules.condition: If two training sessions are scheduled on the same day with any combination of modalities
extracted_rules.action: Follow fixed priority ordering based on goal; default for hybrid athletes: endurance AM + strength PM is acceptable, but strength AM + quality run PM is not
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Priority A (strength-primary): Strength AM -> Endurance PM (if endurance is Z1-Z2 only); Priority B (endurance-primary): Easy run AM -> Strength PM; NEVER: Hard endurance (Z3+) AM -> Strength PM same day
extracted_rules.source: Coffey & Hawley 2017; concurrent training sequencing research consensus
